elements.sent-2411-002: The momentum flow is completely decoupled from the charge current in this regime, since the electrons and holes propagate in opposite directions with exactly equal distribution functions.

elements.sent-2411-007: For the first time, we observe hydrodynamic behavior of the charge current in such simulations, extracting current profiles and a current diffusion coefficient whose temperature dependence qualitatively agrees with predictions from Boltzmann transport theory.

elements.sent-2411-091: We showed that the hydrodynamic behavior of charge current in graphene at CNP can be explained without any coupling to the velocity field.
elements.sent-2411-092: Instead, an equation similar to the NS equations was derived for the charge current from a two-liquid (electrons and holes) Boltzmann formalism.
elements.sent-2411-093: In this case the current diffusion coefficient $\zeta$ substitutes the viscosity.
elements.sent-2411-094: This description is supported by unbiased {\it ab initio QMC simulations, where we clearly observe the hydrodynamic current profiles.}
elements.sent-2411-095: These profiles and $\zeta(T)$, extracted from them, agree qualitatively well with the aforementioned Boltzmann transport theory.
elements.sent-2411-096: As a consequence, the correct interpretation of the observed hydrodynamic profiles of charge current in graphene at CNP should be made in terms of the current diffusion coefficient, and not in terms of viscosity.
elements.sent-2411-097: Beyond the Lorentz-symmetrical case, these results can be extended to any system with particle-hole symmetry at half-filling.

elements.sent-2411-159: In order to quantify the effects caused by adatoms, we first look at the density of states (DoS) for a free tight-binding Hamiltonian (Fig.~\ref{fig:DOSandSigmaOmega (e)).}
elements.sent-2411-160: Adatoms create localized near-zero and high-energy modes.
elements.sent-2411-161: The low-energy modes are located on the sites next to the ones where the adatom is attached, whereas the high-energy states belong to the adatoms themselves.
elements.sent-2411-162: These low-energy modes might be actually responsible for the inverse boundary conditions we already observed for the optical conductivity in the free case.
elements.sent-2411-163: In order to understand this further, we computed the frequency-dependent free conductivity (see Fig.~\ref{fig:DOSandSigmaOmega (a) and (b)) for a lattice of 27 stripes (i.e. 648 orbitals) and the disorder configuration with 8 (2) adatoms on the edge (next-to-the-edge) stripes.}
elements.sent-2411-164: Figure \ref{fig:DOSandSigmaOmega (a) reveals a strong dependence of the profile on the frequency $\omega$.}
elements.sent-2411-165: The bulk conductivity is maximal at low frequencies $\omega \leq 0.2t$.
elements.sent-2411-166: This corresponds to the Drude peak (see (b)) which contributes significantly to the midpoint of the current correlator.
elements.sent-2411-167: The midpoint approximation \eqref{eq:sm:middle_point is therefore no longer valid and we expect inadequate results for an ideal non-interacting system.}
elements.sent-2411-168: At larger frequencies around the Dirac-plateau regime $0.2t < \omega < 1.5t $, the profile is inverted, and the bulk conductivity reaches its minimal value whereas the edge conductivity is bigger.
elements.sent-2411-169: At $\omega \approx 1.8t$, the van Hove singularity results again in a higher conductivity in the bulk.

elements.sent-2411-207: Within the framework of our microscopic description of graphene, we can distinguish between three types of electronic transport mechanisms, depending on the parameters of the microscopic Hamiltonian, temperature and lattice size.
elements.sent-2411-208: Hydrodynamic regime, discussed extensively in this work, occurs when momentum conserving collisions between electrons dominate over all other scattering processes.
elements.sent-2411-209: In this regime, the conductivity profiles follow the continuum description from kinetic theory, despite being measured in QMC on a discrete grid, the lattice.
elements.sent-2411-210: The hydrodynamic description is only valid if the electronic scattering length $l_{\mathrm{ee }$ is significantly smaller than the width $W$ of the samples.}

elements.sent-2411-324: We demonstrated the emergence of hydrodynamic charge transport in freestanding graphene at CNP in the absence of momentum flow.
elements.sent-2411-325: First, we obtained charge current profiles using unbiased QMC simulations in a controlled environment, where momentum-conserving particle-particle collisions are the sole scattering mechanism in the bulk, while diffusive scattering is confined to the edges to establish boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-326: Second, we developed a description for the charge current based on a two-fluid (electrons and holes) Boltzmann formalism.
elements.sent-2411-327: This description leads to NS-type equations written directly for the charge current with the current diffusion coefficient $\zeta$ replacing viscosity.
elements.sent-2411-328: The current profiles obtained from QMC align well with the solutions of these equations, allowing us to extract the temperature dependence of $\zeta(T)$ from these fits.
elements.sent-2411-329: These results show qualitative agreement with Boltzmann transport theory.
elements.sent-2411-330: We expect a similar picture for any system with particle-hole symmetry at half-filling, e.g. for AB-stacked bilayer graphene with quadratic band touching or for any narrow-gap semiconductor with symmetric bands.
elements.sent-2411-331: Another interesting question is the numerical accuracy of the transport coefficients obtained from the Boltzmann equation.
elements.sent-2411-332: Our paper demonstrates, how QMC can serve as a replacement of experiments in sourcing transport data to check and improve approximations used in the kinetic theory.
elements.sent-2411-333: The main advantage of this technique is the precise control over scattering mechanisms in microscopic simulations.
elements.sent-2411-334: Using this approach, we are planning to further investigate possible deficiencies in the computation of the collision integral for strongly correlated quantum systems.
